title: Wall Street Crash (Black Tuesday)
description: Stock prices collapse on the New York Stock Exchange, wiping out billions in market value and marking the onset of the Great Depression.

title: Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act signed
description: Hoover signs a protectionist tariff raising duties on over 20,000 imported goods despite a petition from 1,000+ economists urging a veto; trading partners retaliate, and global trade falls roughly 65%, deepening the Great Depression.

title: Emergency Banking Act
description: Congress passes emergency legislation within days of Roosevelt's inauguration to stabilize the banking system after a nationwide bank holiday, the opening act of the New Deal.

title: Executive Order 6102 — gold standard exit
description: Roosevelt orders private gold coin, bullion and certificates surrendered to the Federal Reserve at $20.67/oz, taking the US off the domestic gold standard as part of New Deal reflation efforts.

title: Glass-Steagall Act (Banking Act of 1933)
description: Separates commercial from investment banking and creates the FDIC to insure bank deposits, a central pillar of New Deal financial regulation.

title: Social Security Act signed
description: Roosevelt signs New Deal legislation creating old-age benefits, unemployment insurance, and aid for dependents and the disabled, establishing the modern US social-insurance system.

title: Bretton Woods Agreement signed
description: 44 Allied nations meeting in New Hampshire agree on a US dollar/gold-anchored fixed exchange-rate system and create the IMF and World Bank, cementing the dollar's postwar reserve-currency role.

title: Marshall Plan (Economic Cooperation Act) signed
description: Truman signs legislation funding roughly $13.3bn in postwar reconstruction aid to Western Europe, cementing the dollar-centered postwar economic order alongside Bretton Woods.

title: Medicare and Medicaid created
description: Johnson signs the Social Security Amendments of 1965, creating federal health insurance for the elderly (Medicare) and the poor/disabled (Medicaid), a major expansion of the US fiscal welfare state.

title: Nixon imposes domestic wage-price controls
description: Alongside suspending dollar-gold convertibility (the globally significant 'Nixon Shock', recorded separately under GLOBAL), Nixon invokes the Economic Stabilization Act to freeze US wages and prices for 90 days to curb domestic inflation.

title: Energy Policy and Conservation Act — Strategic Petroleum Reserve created
description: Responding to the 1973 oil embargo, Congress establishes the Strategic Petroleum Reserve (up to 1 billion barrels) and CAFE fuel-economy standards, the core of post-embargo US energy-security policy.

title: Volcker's "Saturday Night Special" — Fed shifts to reserve targeting
description: Federal Reserve Chair Paul Volcker announces the FOMC will target bank reserves rather than the federal funds rate directly, opening the monetarist experiment" that drives interest rates toward 20% and breaks 1970s inflation at the cost of the 1981-82 recession."

title: Depository Institutions Deregulation and Monetary Control Act signed
description: Carter signs legislation phasing out Regulation Q interest-rate ceilings on deposits, raising deposit insurance to $100,000, and extending Fed reserve requirements to all depository institutions — the opening act of 1980s financial deregulation.

title: Volcker disinflation — federal funds rate peaks near 20%
description: Federal Reserve under Chair Paul Volcker pushes the federal funds rate to roughly 20% and the prime rate to 21.5%, breaking 1970s inflation at the cost of the 1981-82 recession, in which unemployment peaks above 10%.

title: Economic Recovery Tax Act (Reaganomics tax cuts)
description: Reagan signs a package cutting individual income-tax rates roughly 25% over three years and accelerating business depreciation, the centerpiece of 'Reaganomics'.

title: Garn-St Germain Depository Institutions Act signed
description: Reagan signs legislation further deregulating savings and loan associations, permitting adjustable-rate and nonresidential mortgages, and creating money-market deposit accounts — later linked to the savings-and-loan crisis of the late 1980s.

title: Black Monday stock market crash
description: The Dow Jones Industrial Average falls 22.6% in a single day — its largest one-day percentage drop in history — amid program-trading-driven selling; global markets lose an estimated $1.71 trillion, though US markets recover within two years.

title: FIRREA creates Resolution Trust Corporation
description: Financial Institutions Reform, Recovery, and Enforcement Act responds to the savings-and-loan crisis by creating the Resolution Trust Corporation to close and resolve hundreds of insolvent thrifts.

title: NAFTA takes effect
description: North American Free Trade Agreement among the US, Canada and Mexico eliminates most tariffs, reshaping US manufacturing trade and supply chains.

title: Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act repeals Glass-Steagall barriers
description: Clinton signs legislation removing the 1933 Glass-Steagall separation between commercial banking, investment banking and insurance, enabling the large universal-bank consolidation of the 2000s.

title: Dot-com bubble peaks and bursts
description: NASDAQ Composite peaks at 5,048.62 before a two-year collapse (-78% by October 2002) that wipes out trillions in market value and contributes to the 2001 recession.

title: September 11 terrorist attacks
description: Attacks on the World Trade Center and Pentagon close US equity markets for four trading days (the longest shutdown since 1933); markets fall 11% on reopening, and New York City loses an estimated 430,000 jobs and $30bn+ in output over the following year.

title: US housing bubble peaks
description: Home prices measured by the Case-Shiller index peak in early-to-mid 2006 before a two-year decline; the subsequent surge in subprime defaults and foreclosures becomes the proximate trigger of the 2007-08 financial crisis.

title: Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac placed into conservatorship
description: The Federal Housing Finance Agency places both government-sponsored mortgage giants into conservatorship and the Treasury extends financial support via Senior Preferred Stock Purchase Agreements, one of the largest financial-sector interventions of the crisis.

title: TARP enacted
description: Emergency Economic Stabilization Act creates the $700bn Troubled Asset Relief Program to purchase distressed assets and recapitalize banks during the Global Financial Crisis (see GLOBAL entry for the Lehman collapse that triggered it).
